title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant

The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, Acropora, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

The chart tracks two metrics regarding Acropora corals in the Caribbean from 1950 to 2007. The upper line, labeled "Present," begins at approximately 92% in 1950 and follows a downward trajectory to roughly 20% by 2007. This series drops below 70% around 1965, falls beneath 60% near 1982, and crosses the 30% mark around 1992. The lower line, labeled "Dominant," starts near 79% in 1950 and declines to approximately 8% by the end of the period. This metric dips below 50% around 1955, recovers slightly to touch 60% in the mid-1970s, and then falls sharply after 1980, dropping below 20% around 1988 and reaching a low point near 5% in the early 2000s. Throughout the entire timeline, the "Present" values remain higher than the "Dominant" values, with the gap between the two lines widening significantly after 1980. Both lines exhibit their steepest rates of decline between 1980 and 1995. By 2007, the "Present" line sits just under the 20% grid line, while the "Dominant" line rests slightly above the 0% baseline.
